response.description_long:
Every secret, every workload, accounted for. That's the tagline of Vaultra, an Austin-based startup founded in 2022 by security engineers Alex Fontaine and Ritika Nair. They spent years at a large fintech watching the same postmortem repeat: an old key, an unknown consumer, a scramble to rotate without breaking payment flows. Vaultra is the tool they wished existed.

The problem Vaultra addresses is that machine identities — API keys, service-account tokens, database passwords, signing certificates — now outnumber human ones by orders of magnitude and routinely leak. Hard-coded keys in repositories, tokens shared across services, credentials that outlive their creators. Human identity got SSO, MFA, lifecycle management; the service account that can read your production database got none. Security teams know this, but lack a way to see the problem at its real size and fix it without breaking production.

Vaultra discovers, rotates, and governs these non-human credentials. Its agentless discovery connects to AWS, GCP, Azure, Kubernetes, GitHub, and CI systems, building an inventory of every credential, where it is used, and what it can access. The first inventory typically completes the same afternoon you connect. A risk engine ranks findings by blast radius and exposure: unrotated keys, over-privileged tokens, secrets in code, orphaned credentials, certificates approaching expiry. Findings come with usage traces and permission diffs so engineers can act immediately.

Rotation is designed to not break production. One-click rotation creates a replacement credential, updates every consumer through native integrations (AWS Secrets Manager, GCP Secret Manager, Azure Key Vault, Kubernetes secrets, GitHub Actions, etc.), validates consumers are healthy, then revokes the old credential. A rollback window keeps the old credential revocable-but-restorable, so a rotation that breaks an overlooked consumer becomes an incident of minutes, not an outage. Rotations run one-off, on schedule, or automatically when a finding crosses a severity threshold.

Policies turn cleanup into steady state: maximum credential age, mandatory expiry, least-privilege enforcement that trims grants to observed usage, no-secrets-in-code checks wired into pull requests. Drift is surfaced daily; the quarterly audit becomes an export. Vaultra maps findings to SOC 2 and ISO 27001 controls and exports audit-ready evidence. The platform itself is SOC 2 Type II certified.

Customers are security teams at cloud-native companies from 50 to 5,000 employees. The site cites deployments inventorying over 100,000 machine credentials. The pattern is consistent: teams suspect a few thousand credentials, discovery finds several times that, and the first month is triage. After that, policies hold the line.

Vaultra deploys as SaaS or in your own cloud account. Pricing is annual subscription tiered by number of identities under management, with a discovery-only tier, and rotation and policy tiers. No per-rotation charges. Integrations cover major clouds, Kubernetes, GitHub, secret stores, databases, and alerting tools. The API and Terraform provider expose the same operations as the console.

Founders Alex Fontaine and Ritika Nair lead a team of 38: security engineers, cloud-platform engineers, and former compliance leads. Vaultra's research on granted-versus-used permissions has been presented at practitioner conferences, and its annual state of machine identity report is regularly cited in industry coverage.
